title.en: Tight Feedback Loops: Small Diffs, Fast Checks, Safe Checkpoints

description.en: The speed of vibe coding doesn't come from giant AI leaps — it comes from a loop so tight that mistakes can't hide. Change one small thing, run it, commit when it's green. Learn why a 5-line diff you understand beats a 200-line diff you don't.

story_variants.en:
## Speed Comes From the Loop, Not the Leap

Beginners think vibe coding is fast because the AI writes a lot of code at once. It isn't. The code firehose is the *slow* part — it just feels fast for thirty seconds, right up until something breaks and you have no idea which of the 200 new lines did it.

Real speed comes from the **loop**: the round-trip between making a change and finding out whether it worked. The tighter that loop, the faster you actually ship. Vibe coding is fast when the loop is small, and dangerous when it's big.

### The Big-Bang Loop

Here's the trap. You prompt: *"rewrite the whole file to add caching, retries, and logging."* The AI cheerfully returns 200 lines. You skim it — it looks reasonable — you run it, and:

```
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable
```

Now what? The bug is somewhere in 200 lines you didn't write and didn't read. You can't tell which change caused it, because you made *all* the changes at once. You're not coding anymore — you're excavating. You read the whole dump line by line, the exact work you were trying to skip, except now it's tangled with three other features. The 200-line diff didn't save time. It hid the bug in a haystack and handed you the haystack.

### The Tight Loop

The fix is almost boringly simple. Make the loop small:

1. **Change ONE small thing.** Add caching. Just caching. A diff small enough that you can read and understand every line of it.
2. **Run it.** Immediately. On a real input. Before you touch anything else.
3. **Commit when it's green.** The moment it works, `git commit`. That green state is now a checkpoint you can always return to.
4. **Repeat** for retries, then for logging — each as its own small, run, commit cycle.

When step 3 explodes, you already know the culprit: it's in the *five* lines you just changed, not two hundred. Debugging a 5-line diff is trivial. Debugging a 200-line diff is a career.

| | Big-bang loop | Tight loop |
|---|---|---|
| Diff size | 200 lines at once | 5 lines at a time |
| When it breaks | *Somewhere* in there | The line you just touched |
| Did you read it? | Skimmed it | Understood it |
| Rollback | Untangle by hand | `git reset` to last green |
| Feels | Fast, then awful | Steady, then done |

### Commits Are Checkpoints, Not Paperwork

The most underrated tool in vibe coding is `git commit`. Every time the code is green — works, runs, does the thing — commit it. You're not documenting for some future historian; you're dropping a save point in a video game. When the next change goes wrong (and one will), you don't debug the tangle. You `git reset --hard` to the last green commit and try a smaller step. Rolling back to working code is almost always cheaper than debugging your way out of a mess.

This is why "commit often" isn't a discipline chore — it's what *lets* you move fast. A tight loop without checkpoints is a tightrope without a net. With checkpoints, every experiment is safe: worst case, you lose five lines of work, not an afternoon.

### Why Huge AI Dumps Are Dangerous

It bears repeating, because the AI makes it so easy: asking for a giant rewrite feels efficient and is the opposite. A change you didn't read is a change you can't trust, and a 200-line change is one you *won't* read. The bug isn't gone — it's just hidden in code you've never looked at. Small diffs aren't a limitation you tolerate. They're the whole technique.

> **Key idea:** Vibe coding is fast because the feedback loop is tight, not because the leaps are big. Change one small thing, run it immediately, and commit every green state. A 5-line diff you understand and can roll back beats a 200-line diff you skimmed and can't — every single time.

left.label: Big-bang loop: 200-line dump, bug hidden in a haystack you didn't read

right.label: Tight loop: 5-line diff, run it, commit green, roll back anytime

description.en: Speed comes from the size of the loop, not the size of the leap. Small diff, fast run, safe checkpoint.

animation_sequence.code:
total = 0
for n in [10, 20, 30, 40]:
    total = n      # buggy step: '=' not '+='
print(total)

executionSteps.description: Each pass OVERWRITES total instead of adding to it.
Wrong: we wanted 100. One small step is broken — and it's easy to spot because it's the only change.

output: Speed comes from the loop, not the leap.
Change one small thing. Run it. Commit when green.
A 5-line diff you understand beats a 200-line diff you don't.

instruction.en: A small 3-step pipeline turns "10,20,30,40" into its final running total, which should be 100. But the output is wrong. Don't rewrite the whole pipeline — that's the big-bang trap. Be the tight-loop coder: check each step in isolation, find the ONE broken step, and fix just that step. step1_parse and step3_take_last are correct; the bug is a single wrong operator in step2_running_sum. With the given input the pipeline must print 100. Expected output: 100

practice_challenges.starter_code:
# A 3-step pipeline. ONE step has a single-character bug.
# Don't rewrite everything — isolate the broken step and fix only it.

def step1_parse(raw):
    # Correct: split "10,20,30,40" into [10, 20, 30, 40].
    return [int(x) for x in raw.split(",")]

def step2_running_sum(nums):
    # Should return the cumulative running total:
    # [10, 20, 30, 40] -> [10, 30, 60, 100]
    out = []
    total = 0
    for n in nums:
        total = n      # BUG: this overwrites total instead of adding to it
        out.append(total)
    return out

def step3_take_last(running):
    # Correct: the final running total is the last element.
    return running[-1]

def pipeline(raw):
    parsed = step1_parse(raw)
    running = step2_running_sum(parsed)
    return step3_take_last(running)

print(pipeline("10,20,30,40"))

en: Isolate each step. Run step1_parse("10,20,30,40") — it correctly gives [10, 20, 30, 40]. So step 1 is fine.
Run step3_take_last([10, 30, 60, 100]) — it correctly gives 100. So step 3 is fine. The bug must be in step2.
Look at step2_running_sum: 'total = n' replaces total each pass, so you only ever get the last number. A running sum must ADD.
Change 'total = n' to 'total += n'. Don't touch the working steps — small diff, then re-run.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert step1_parse("10,20,30,40") == [10, 20, 30, 40]
assert step2_running_sum([10, 20, 30, 40]) == [10, 30, 60, 100]
assert step3_take_last([10, 30, 60, 100]) == 100
assert pipeline("10,20,30,40") == 100
assert pipeline("5") == 5

q.en: Where does the real speed of vibe coding come from?

en: Asking the AI for as much code as possible at once
A tight feedback loop: small change, fast run, frequent commit
Never running the code until the feature is fully done
Writing 200-line diffs so you make fewer requests

q.en: Why is a 200-line AI dump more dangerous than five small diffs?

en: It uses more tokens
When it breaks, the bug is hidden somewhere in code you didn't read and can't isolate
Big diffs are always syntactically invalid
The AI charges more for longer outputs

q.en: Why commit at every green (working) state?

en: To satisfy a manager's paperwork rules
It creates a checkpoint you can roll back to instead of debugging a tangle
Commits make the code run faster
So the AI can read your git history

q.en: Your tight-loop change just broke the build. Where is the bug almost certainly hiding?

en: Randomly anywhere in the whole codebase
In the small diff you just made and ran — that's why you run after each change
In a file you haven't opened in weeks
It is impossible to tell
